title: The Actuary Page
subtitle: What the math says about survivors

content.text: Recovered internal circulation: contractor mortality review, actuarial division. Reprinted as received.
SUBJECT: Survivorship beyond the sixth scar event. FINDING: None projected. RECOMMENDATION: See addendum.

This is how the corps count you. Nothing below changes a rule — the roll the actuaries are describing lives on The Staircase — but every contractor who works long enough eventually hears their own number read back to them.
At six recorded scar events, survivorship ceases to be an underwriting question. The division has no instrument for pricing what remains, and declines to acquire one.

Six scars deep, the saying in the bars goes, survival is a natural 20. The saying is not wrong: by the sixth untreated scar the Fate arithmetic has climbed to where only the top face always answers (see The Staircase).
Past six the table has no row. A contractor carrying seven or more scars is a person the math says should not exist. The math is written by people who have never had to buy one of them a drink.

NOTICE OF RECLASSIFICATION. Contractors recording a seventh scar event are removed from standard mortality tables and flagged for manual review. They are not an error in the data; they are a hole in it. Underwriting is suspended. Employment is not.
they have a filing category for people who refuse to die on schedule. of course they do.

Bars count scars like tours. Nobody asks how many fights you have walked into — they ask how many times you have come back from being gone. The corps count the same ledger; the difference is that the bar buys a round and the corp opens a file.
One policy in the world actually pays out on a Fate Roll, and no actuary writes it — see Influence.

content.label: NEW BLOOD
content.text: When a count ends, replacement characters — when and how new blood enters the squad — are handled GM-side. See the GM Core.
ADDENDUM: Flagged contractors remain fully insurable with respect to equipment, vehicles, and third-party liability. The division wishes them a long career and declines to estimate one.
read it twice. the van is covered. you are a rounding error with a name.
